sentences.text: --St.
Nicholas, as I've told you, its 5 of December every year.
[PAUSES] So on the 4 of December, as a pure coincidence, is the day before St.
Nicholas, obviously.
And as children, even in the camp, we were looking forward to Sinterklaas.
That's how Dutch children say 'St.
Nicholas,' Sinterklaas.

[PAUSES] On the 4 of December, apparently all the diamond transport men met in one barrack and made some decision which had tremendous consequences to all of us, which I did not know what those decisions were at that time.
I do know that very soon after the meeting where all the men attended, all the men were put on transport.
[PAUSES] All the men that had been at the meeting, all diamond workers, all to do with the diamond business, were put on transport.
[PAUSES] I have now in my possession the original German lists, copies, photocopies, of the original German transport list.
[PAUSES] The men were ail sent out of Belsen.
I, my mother, nor my sister, or any of the children, or their mothers, for the most part-- with an exception of about three men-- never saw our fathers, or the women their husbands, again.
Ever.
[PAUSES] The following day, that was the 5th of December, on the day of St.
Nicholas, all the women and older children were taken out of where we were, that part, i.e., the Sternlager, to another part of the camp.
[PAUSES] It was a bright day that day, I remember.
Cold, but a bright day.
And we were all standing, and all of a sudden, the children were told to go to a separate place, a distance, I would say, of no more, maybe, that 30 yards away from the mothers.
Had I been 13 in 1944, which I was not, I would have gone with my father, because most boys 13, 15, 16 went with the fathers.
I went with my mother because I was still under 13, as did my sister, as did the other children.
We were separated physically from our mothers.
[PAUSES] And then all the mothers were marched off.
And that was the last time I saw my mother, or, indeed, the other children saw their mothers.
[PAUSES] We were left where we were.
I always remember there was straw.
We could sit down, the children.
And there were around about 60 children.
We were just left there.
There were no guards, there were no-- there was nobody.
We were just left there, still within the confines of the camp, but not within the confines of the camp within the camp where we had been known as the Sternlager.
No, outside.
It was like-- it was like-- when I say, 'outside,' we were outside like what you would call, like, a barn.
But it was open.
We were, like, outside, but it was the barn.
I don't know whether it had any agricultural connection or-- it was like a barn.
And we were there.
I don't know how long we sat there.
We probably sat there the whole day.
There was nobody there.
Nobody around.
We never got anything.
We just sat there.
As far as my memory goes, later that evening [PAUSES] we were put in a lorry, and they drove us off through the pine trees.
I don't know where we went.
I don't know how long it took.
And then all of a sudden, we found ourselves off the truck, off the lorry, again in a group outside of a barrack.
A barrack that was in a completely separate part of the camp, where I'd never been.
And we were told to go in that barrack.
And then we found ourselves bunks and laid down.
And a couple of women-- older women who weren't Dutch, who didn't speak Dutch, and we only spoke Dutch, because we were Dutch-- they kind of started-- how could I say?
Like, getting busy with us.
You know, like, helping us take our clothes off and laying down or saying, go in that bunk.
And that's-- that day and the day before, I think are the worst days, up to then, in my life, when my sister and I were left alone with the other children.
And not with our parents and not with any people that we knew.
Not with any people from Holland.
Only the children.
As the women I saw-- there may be been two or three women-- they were not Dutch.
They were either Polish or German.
At that time I didn't know what they were, but I knew they weren't Dutch.
They didn't speak Dutch.
Only Dutch people speak Dutch.
[PAUSES] And from that time, I never saw my father ever again.
And at that time, I never saw my mother.
I then didn't see my aunt anymore.
I didn't see my cousin anymore.
I was just with Helen, my sister, and the children.
Yeah, we were together then all the time.
We were together all the time.
Her bunk was like that, and my bunk-- I think my bunk was underneath, which was lower to the floor.
And if I remember, in that barrack, we only had two, not three.
[PAUSES] I'm not-- can't quite remember exactly when it happened, but the was a time in the camp, in the beginning of 1945, when lots of women arrived in that camp.
Lots of women with different nationalities-- Hungarian women, French women, North African women that spoke French.
Because I remember at that time the French women singing always a song.
It's called [SPEAKING FRENCH].
It's a very old-fashioned French song.
I remember that.
And the Russian women.
And I always remembered, all my life, that melody.
A Russian-- which I found out, like, six months ago-- it's a Russian melody.
It's not a Jewish melody.
But it is Russian melody.
And I always remember the women singing that.
I think when that started to happen, when more and more people came into the camp, the conditions of Belsen started to deteriorate very, very badly.
I could sing the melody, but I don't know its words, because I can't speak Russian.
The melody-- I know Russian people know it.
And it goes-- [SINGING 'KATUSHA'] You know, that has stuck in my mind 50 years. 50 years.
No.
I know now, but I didn't know then.
And I think--
It's a song about a girl singing about her boyfriend that's gone off to the wars.
This was explained to me about six months ago.
Afraid.
Afraid.
That I wouldn't see my mother again.
[PAUSES] Afraid that I wouldn't see my mother again.
Both cried.
All the children were crying.
All the children were crying, and the older ones tried to comfort the younger ones.
Some them was two years old, some of those children.
Those children.

[PAUSES] We stayed basically in the barrack, and as became known to me and the other children at that time, there was a Polish woman-- one of those women that started doing with us when we were first told go to the barrack, turned out to be a Polish woman who to me, when I was 12, seemed an old lady.
Old.
She was, at that time, probably 27, 28 years old.
But to a boy of 12, that's old.
[PAUSES] That woman started looking after us, started somehow mysteriously bringing food to us.
And I knew her by the name of Luba.
L-U-B-A.
That's all I knew about her, that this woman, Luba, seemed to be in charge of us, seemed to concern herself with us, and bring food.
[PAUSES] Soup, which, you know, like, watery kind of soup.
Sometimes bread.
And we did have, at that time, I remember so distinctly, sometimes white bread.
Now, white bread in the war is like-- was like gold dust.
First of all, bread is like gold dust.
But then to get it white is even better than gold dust, because the bread we had gotten in Belsen, [PAUSES] and not on a very regular basis, was the German army bread, the kriegs.
So white bread was something very special.
And this woman, of course, forged with me personally, through my personal experience, and with the other children, a very special relationship.
That woman became to us like our mother.
[PAUSES] That time, we had-- one time I remember-- and it happened on various occasions-- I would look over, up in the sky, and I would see, high above sky, trails of aircraft.
Although we never got any news in the camp, we always surmised things were bad for the Germans because of their behavior towards us.
The worse they were, the worse we thought was their military campaign.
And we'd see these trails of aircraft.
I mean hundreds, literally hundreds, in daylight.
Hundreds of these trials of aircraft, you know, the vapor of trails.
And one time I remember distinctly there was a dogfight above the camp, and a few people got hit in the camp.
And I remember some of the barracks were shot up.
A dogfight, that's between opposing aircraft, you know, trying to shoot each other down.
And it became such a low-level dogfight that it impacted the camp with stray bullets.
And I believe some adults did get hit.
So we knew by that time things were going against the Germans.
So we started to feel maybe, as children-- and mostly I say to you the ones that were well were still playing-- we thought maybe we would get out.
Maybe something would happen.
We would get out.
We could go home.
Some of the children's health started to deteriorate, because by that time, our nutrition was a lot worse than what it had been.
Although we were getting from Luba food, we suffered more nutritionally.
The weaker children, I think, got weak.
And then, of course, we got lice.
And from the lice, you get typhus.
I personally had typhus.
I think most of the children had typhus.
We still had all our own clothes, the original clothes.
I still had that coat that I'd brought from England with my grandmother in 1939 to Holland.
I still had my old ski boots.
My sister got ill.
My sister couldn't eat hardly anything anymore.
She would drink a bit of water.
I would sit next to her bunk trying to feed her the soup.
And she would say to me, I-- you eat it.
[PAUSES] And I have a big problem with that to this day.
Psychologically, I have a big problem with that.
And I know it's unfounded that I should have the problem, because it's not logical.
I have the problem of I eat the soup which she refused to eat.
[PAUSES] And in a kind of crazy meshuggeneh way, I say to myself, that's why-- what happened to her is because I ate her soup.
I know, logically speaking, that is not so.
And I don't say that to convince myself of that.
I know that is logically not so.
But when I think and see myself in my mind's eye sitting next to the bunk feeding my poor sister the food, which she just couldn't eat it.
She couldn't get it down the throat.
I could still eat, although I'd lost a lot of weight because of the typhus.
The lack of food, the lack of proper food-- vegetables, fruit, was nonexistent-- I had deteriorated physically, although I was still up and about, so to speak.
[PAUSES] That time in the camp when we were outside of the barracks, there were an awful lot of people-- corpses.
The barrack we were was amongst other barracks, and it seemed to me that every morning, people would take dead people out of the barracks and put them outside.
Like, lay them down.
So in the beginning of 1945, after being separated from my mother and my father, the barracks around us all had, like, heaps of bodies.
Now, a young boy, and the young-- other young people-- I think the eldest person, the eldest child, was maybe 15 or 16.
And she was a girl.
That's why she didn't go with her father, because she was a girl.
[PAUSES]
Her name is Hettie.
Hettie [INAUDIBLE] is her name.
[PAUSES] It's terrible to see corpses when you're an adult.
It's certainly terrible to see emaciated corpses when you're an adult.
But when you see that as a child, that is so horrific.
That is so burning into your very being.
That-- personally speaking, and I'm probably speaking for others-- that visual memory of that, you are never without, no matter what you do.
No matter if you're having a nice time or not a nice time, the memory, that visual picture in your mind's memory, is with you, I believe, till the day you die.
That is so horrific.
No, not people I personally knew.
There were men and women, naked, because they're clothes had been taken by the living.
I've seen people with their thighs cut off to eat the food.
And that for, you know, I was, let me say, at that time sexually innocent.
To see naked women and naked men, more skin than bone, like-- almost like dolls with disheveled hair, with the deep-set sockets or eyes, is horrible.
It's terrible.
It is really terrible.
It's something that has been terrible with me all my life.
[PAUSES]
So as the months went by in the beginning of '45, these-- there were more and more.
More and more.
And, of course, the camp stank, stank of death, because the decomposition that starts taking place quite quickly is terrible, terrible, terrible.
And we left with that all the-- I mean, it was like-- we were eating and breathing this-- this-- this smell, which it-- it-- it-- encompassed, I'm sure, the whole camp.
And on a windy day, must have gone outside.
[PAUSES]
It was awful, really.
No, I didn't know where my mother was.
I didn't know where my father was.
But at that time, I was able to go to the barbed wire and see my aunt.
I saw my aunt and her son, my cousin, John.
We would talk through the barbed wire.
I would stand there with my sister, holding her hand, talk to my aunt.
My cousin, in 1944, was five years old, because he was born in '39.
We would speak in Dutch until, at different times, a kapo would come on her side of the camp, on her side of the wire, and chase her off.
Yes, it was all right.
It was within the camp.
It wasn't an electrified fence within the camp.
The electrified fences are-- were the perimeter of the camp.
There were two sets of fences.
One was not electrified, and the next-- and then there was a no man's land in between where sometimes the Germans would walk with dogs, other than the ones in the watch towers.
And the outside fence was the electrified fence.
But the wire in the camp was not.
[PAUSES] The-- one day I went to the wire, because I went every day with my sister to see my aunt, she wasn't there anymore.
[PAUSES] She wasn't there.
And I went back a few days, every day, to see, and she never came back.
She never came back to talk to me.
[PAUSES] That was a bad time for me, because she was all I had, except my sister, of my family.
And the other children, but they weren't my family.
[PAUSES] Unfortunately, my aunt died a few years ago in England.
But I was always close with my aunt because of that.
And I'm close to my cousin that lives in London.
The one that used to be there with his mother, my aunt.
But basically, it was terrible that my aunt was gone, because my aunt was my connection to England, freedom.
It was connection to my mother, to my grandmother, to my great-grandmother, to my uncles and aunts, and to my life in Holland before the war.
[PAUSES] Awful.
I know 'awful' is not the right word, but it's-- but you know what I mean.
So we stayed in the barrack with Luba, the other children.
[PAUSES] But it was-- the-- the situation with the-- with the children deteriorated a lot with the lice.
Terrible with the lice.
Terrible.
We used to pick them out of each other's hair and put them between our thumbnails and squash them.
During the epidemic of--
Typhus.
To my knowledge-- to my knowledge-- there were about 60-odd children, diamond transfer children.
To my knowledge, none of them died at that time.
To my knowledge.
But I'm not sure about that.
I'm really not sure about that.
We stayed there.
And then we could hear, you know, gun-- cannon fire.
We could hear in the distance.
And we knew that we were going to be liberated.
[PAUSES] And on the morning of the 15th of April, 1945, we saw tanks coming into the camp.
Not many.
And what I found out, English soldiers and Canadian soldiers.
And [PAUSES] soon they-- and they-- they had a lot of food.
They gave food, [PAUSES] the army ration food.
And Luba took charge of all that, to dole out what she must've thought was right for us and what was not right for us.
And then-- I spoke a few words of English, not many.
[PAUSES] I remember people came into the barrack where we were.
A British cameraman came in the barrack, took films and/or photographs in the barrack.
Took a photograph of my sister, [PAUSES] amongst others.
And [PAUSES] being-- I was 13 years old then, because I'm born March.
I was liberated a month after my birthday.
I was not bar mitzvahed, because there was no bar mitzvah in the camp.
And I spoke-- I went up to a few of the soldiers and asked if they knew my uncles, because my uncles were in England.
And I thought by that time my uncles must've been in the military.
I was 13 year old.
And I said, do you know my Uncle Sam Rabbie?
Do you know Michael Rabbie?
Do you know them?
No, no, no, no.
Of course, I had no idea what a vast apparatus an army is, you know at that time.
We were then-- after we were liberated, a few days afterwards, we were taken to a place-- all the children-- in the camp still, and we were told to take all our clothes off.
We took all the clothes off, and they had, like, insect sprays, and they sprayed us with white powder, which I then knew was DDT.
And we were sprayed all over.
Like, we came, like-- like zombies, all white with the DDT.
My sister was still able to walk.
We never saw our clothes again.
I never saw my coat or my boots again, or the other clothes.
Nor did any of us saw our clothes again.
We were then transferred out of the camp in army Red Cross trucks that had a big red cross painted on the outside.
We were huddled in there in a few trucks like that.
And we were taken outside of Belsen to what was then the German army barracks, what is now, still today, 1996, the British army base.
We were taken there into one of the holes which they turned into a hospital.
And I was put into a hospital bed.
I never saw Luba again after we were transported to the hospital.
I didn't know what happened to Luba after that time.
All I knew, that I was in the bed, in a hospital, was all white, was iron bedsteads, and there were other children of my group in that room.
That's what I remember.
But I don't remember anything about Luba after we were taken out to be brought to the hospital at all.
Oh, I knew nothing about my mother.
I knew nothing about my mother.
I knew nothing about my father, my mother, my aunt, my cousin, my grandparents.
Nobody.
I knew that it was my sister and me and the other Dutch children.
That's what I knew.
I don't remember what they did to me in the hospital.
The only thing I remember that stands out are two things in the hospital.
First of all, a nurse came to my bed.
[PAUSES] She was Dutch, but she was in the Dutch Red Cross part of the British army.
OK.
